inline_comments.message: hate to be picky, but I think is important to keep style consistency on a per file basis once started in a style, if you don't mind no " " for parameters provided that this file uses that approach everywhere else.
Naming convention followed here is to have "AccessOut" , "Out" as a suffix for the appender
follow same convention in file name patterns .. either change this, or change all the other ones, note "%i.log.zip" vs ".log.%i.zip"

this number should be < than networkOut size, as the network.out populates all dmaap messages, and they are much more that the ones that would go through the embedded jetty servers.

This brings the question of the "network.log" should be used instead of "access.log" to log this kind of information.   I'd like to hear your opinion on it.  Point being this is just additional network output, and very, very small in volume, and just for embedded jetty http servers".

One alternative as well could be to have this log separated but with a more specific name, such http-server.log ..

What are your thoughts?

can you attach a sample line of what the output would look like to get a feel on what it looks like?

In addition, if you don't mind to move this pattern to the top of the file to be consistent with other pattern arrangements in this file.
If you can follow same approach and have an "Async" logger similar to the other loggers
one question Jim for my own knowledge, does jetty server use by name this "org.eclipse.jetty.server.RequestLog" logger as its name, just if you know, not that important ..

It seems that the config file is inconsistent.  This name followed the pattern for the network log.  Nevertheless, the name is now modified to match the others - we may want to fix the name for the network log to be consistent.

Changed to 1MB.

Regarding network.log, I wondered the same thing.  Note, however, that the "access.log" is a standard for all web servers.  As a result, that is where the average person would expect to find the information.

Note: this follows the standard access.log format for all web servers, and it is literally hard-coded into the jetty Slf4jRequestLog class.

127.0.0.1 - - [23/Aug/2017:17:22:49 +0000] "GET //localhost:8080/ HTTP/1.1" 200 22 
127.0.0.1 - - [23/Aug/2017:17:22:49 +0000] "GET //localhost:8080/favicon.ico HTTP/1.1" 200 22 
127.0.0.1 - - [23/Aug/2017:17:22:49 +0000] "GET //localhost:8080/favicon.ico HTTP/1.1" 200 22

This seems dangerous to me for two reasons:
1) If the async queue becomes full, then the async logger drops messages - something we want to avoid if there's an actual security event that we're trying to investigate
2) If the JVM crashes (or is killed) while messages are still in the queue, then they will be lost - again, something we want to avoid where security logs are concerned
Yes, that's the name that the jetty logger uses.

Thanks, can you please do this change in network log to keep it consistent?

Can you also do the same in other log file?

I'm having trouble with the word 'access' and the  ambiguity it introduces with 'network' (log), in theory much of the 'network' traffic is "access" but none of that goes there, same thing within control loops same argument for other types of traffic.  PDP-D is not a web server, and different in nature, with http server functionality secondary and optional and with limited scope..   Traditional web servers will record all of incoming http requests in their access log, but in pdp-d most of the traffic will be reflected in network.log as it offers other networking endpoint alternatives currently preferred.

Is it possible to come up with a name that nails down a little more semantically the reduced scope of what this log is logging?

OK, can you add the same "AsyncAccessOut" just to be consistent even and still point your logger to "AccessOut" (the synchronous one)?

I think this may be a bigger general discussion not only applicable to this.   One could argue that if the queue for this logger, which should have very little traffic is full, chances are is that we've been (D)DoS'ed, and we are entertaining those requests on being dropped.   Not saying that you have a very valid point, in any case if you can leave an asynchronous entry as the other ones, so we can switch appender quickly if we want to.
